I used to freeze when someone asked a hard question.
I used to stay silent in meetings.
I didn't use to ask questions.
I used to be nervous, but now I stay calm.
Vanessa, this is lesson twenty. How do you feel about your English now?
Honestly, I'm proud. I used to freeze when someone asked me a question.
I remember. And in meetings?
I used to stay silent and just nod. But now I share my opinions and ask questions.
That's real progress. What are you most proud of?
That I overcame the fear. It took courage to keep going, and I'm grateful for every step.
You've accomplished so much. This milestone is only the beginning, isn't it?
It is. I used to dream about speaking like this. Now I just do it.
I used to freeze, but now I stay calm.
I'm proud of the progress I've made.
I've overcome the fear of speaking.
I'm grateful for every step of this journey.
This milestone is only the beginning.

Let me tell you how far I have come. When I started this journey, I used to be terrified of speaking English. I used to write every sentence in my head first, and by the time it was ready, the moment had passed. In meetings, I used to stay silent and just nod, even when I had something to say. I did not use to ask questions, because I was afraid of sounding wrong. But things are different now. I do not freeze anymore. When I am unsure, I buy a little time and give an honest estimate. When there is a problem, I apologize calmly and explain what I should have done. I make plans, I share my opinions, and I describe things the way I see them. I am not perfect, and I do not need to be. What I feel now is pride. I am proud of the progress I have made, grateful for every small step, and I have the courage to keep going. This is a real milestone, and it is only the beginning.

I want to say something about growth, because I think people get it wrong. They imagine that fluency arrives one morning, like a switch. It does not. Growth is quiet. It is the difference between who you used to be and who you are today, and most of the time you do not even notice it happening. Think about it. A few months ago, you used to avoid certain conversations. You used to plan your escape from a phone call. Now you handle those same moments without thinking twice. That is not luck. That is the result of showing up, again and again, even when it was hard. So here is my advice. Do not measure yourself against perfect English. Measure yourself against the person you used to be. When you do that, the progress is impossible to miss. You have overcome more than you realize, and you should be proud. Every milestone you reach is proof that the next one is possible too.

First, I look back at where I started and remember how I used to be afraid to speak. Then I notice the small steps, the progress I made one lesson at a time. After that, I name what changed: I used to freeze, but now I stay calm and give an honest answer. Next, I feel grateful for the growth and proud of how far I have come. In the end, I find the courage to keep going, because this milestone is only the beginning.
